item.title: Epistemic Universalism
item.description: Epistemic universalism is the assumption that for knowledge to be legitimate, it must be articulable in terms that any rational agent could, in principle, accept. It grounds validity in universality: a claim is warranted only if the criteria supporting it are available to everyone, regardless of position, culture, or relational context. This appears as a protection against arbitrary authority — no one is bound by claims they cannot evaluate on shared terms. In practice, it functions as a gatekeeping mechanism that delegitimizes every form of knowing that depends on particular relations, places, or practices.

item.title: Teleology
item.description:
Teleology is explanation or organization structured by an end. A teleological system is one where purpose precedes action — where what something is for determines what it does, what counts as success, and what counts as deviation. The acorn grows toward the oak. The policy is evaluated by whether it achieves its stated goal. The community is organized toward the society it intends to become.

The concept originates in Aristotle’s four causes, where the telos (final cause) of a thing is its purpose or end-state. For Aristotle, understanding a thing means understanding what it is for: the knife exists to cut, the eye exists to see, the polis exists to enable the good life. This framework dominated Western natural philosophy until the early modern period, when mechanistic explanation displaced final causes in physics and biology. Darwin’s theory of natural selection provided a non-teleological account of biological adaptation: organisms are not designed for their environments; they are shaped by them, through differential survival, without intention or purpose.

item.title: Polyvalence
item.description:
Polyvalence is the condition of bearing multiple values, meanings, or interpretations simultaneously. A polyvalent sign, concept, or structure does not resolve into a single determinate meaning but sustains several at once — not as ambiguity (failure to determine) but as constitutive multiplicity. The sign that is polyvalent is not broken or vague. It is doing what signs do: meaning differently in different contexts while remaining recognizably the same sign.

In semiotics, polyvalence describes signs whose meaning cannot be reduced to a single referent or interpretation. A symbol may function differently in different contexts — the cross means different things in a church, a hospital, and a military cemetery — while remaining the same sign. Its polyvalence is not noise to be filtered out but a feature of how signification works. This is the fundamental distinction from ambiguity. An ambiguous sign has one intended meaning and others that are parasitic. A polyvalent sign has multiple meanings that are all genuinely its own.
